summary: Big moves on the BeltLine today as Reynoldstown's long-delayed townhomes finally break ground while a sweet new food hall preps to open in East Lake. Meanwhile, Atlantans are dealing with chaos at Hartsfield-Jackson as TSA agents work without pay and ICE shows up to help (sort of).

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Long-Delayed BeltLine Townhomes Finally Break Ground
topStory.summary: After sitting as empty lots for nearly five years, that corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown is finally seeing action. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is moving forward with 85 residential units just a block from the BeltLine's Eastside Trail. The project had been stalled since the original buildings were cleared, leaving neighbors wondering what would happen to that prime real estate so close to the trail.
topStory.body:
You know that corner in Reynoldstown where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace? The one that's been sitting empty for what feels like forever? Well, grab your hard hat because things are finally happening. Embry Development has taken over what was originally Toll Brothers' project and is moving forward with 85 townhomes and condos just a stone's throw from the BeltLine.

The site has been cleared since 2021, becoming one of those neighborhood eyesores that makes you wonder if anything will ever actually get built. Embry bought the 2-acre site from Toll Brothers two years ago, inheriting all the permits and approvals but apparently needing time to get their ducks in a row. Now they're calling it Camber Crossing and promising the first units will hit the market soon.

This is prime BeltLine real estate we're talking about. Being just a block west of the Eastside Trail means these units will likely sell fast, especially given how popular that stretch has become. The question is whether they'll price out the longtime residents who've been watching this saga unfold from their front porches.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction equipment at the Reynoldstown development site

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 'Missing Middle' Housing Village for Under $700K
stories.summary: The Cottages at Midway is launching next week as Decatur's answer to the affordability crisis. With 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards, it's designed to offer something between apartments and million-dollar homes. When median home values in Decatur are pushing $700K, this project aims to give families a shot at homeownership without leaving the city.
stories.body:
Decatur's housing market has gone completely bananas, with median home values north of $700,000. But the Cottages at Midway on Midway Road might be the breath of fresh air families have been waiting for. This isn't your typical subdivision - we're talking about 18 houses arranged around shared courtyards and green space, giving you that village feel without the village price tag.

The 2.1-acre development is replacing two underused lots with what planners call 'missing middle' housing. Think of it as the sweet spot between cramped apartments and those massive houses that only tech executives can afford. The soft launch starts next week, so if you've been priced out of Decatur proper, this might be your chance to get back in.

What's smart about this project is the layout. Instead of cramming as many houses as possible onto the lots, they've created shared spaces that encourage neighbors to actually know each other. It's the kind of community-focused development that Decatur has always been about, just scaled for today's reality.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing the layout of Cottages at Midway development

stories.headline: West End's New BeltLine Townhomes Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is finally rising up along the BeltLine's Southwest corridor. Ackerman & Co. started demolition a year ago and now has 23 townhomes planned, with the first seven units nearing completion. It's rare to find new for-sale townhomes along this stretch of the BeltLine, making this a noteworthy addition to the West End's housing options.
stories.body:
Remember when Ackerman & Co. started tearing down buildings at 600 Hopkins Street last year? Well, those 23 townhomes they promised are actually taking shape, and they've got a new name: GW Parkside. The first seven units are getting close to move-in ready, with marketing materials promising a spring debut.

What makes this project interesting is how rare new for-sale townhomes are along the Southwest BeltLine. Most of the action has been on the Eastside Trail, so seeing Ackerman (the same folks behind Lee + White) invest in this stretch shows confidence in the area's trajectory. These aren't going to be cheap, but they're filling a gap in the market for people who want to be BeltLine-adjacent without paying Inman Park prices.

The timing is pretty good too. The Southwest trail has been getting more love lately, and with Lee + White continuing to expand their food and retail offerings nearby, this whole area is starting to feel less like a hidden gem and more like the next big thing.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End

stories.headline: Gansevoort Market Creator Opens East Lake Food Hall in April
stories.summary: Robert Montwaid, the mastermind behind NYC's Gansevoort Market, is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to East Lake. Vela Park Food Works opens mid-April with six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining. It's part of the new Vela Park rental community near East Lake Golf Club, adding another high-end food destination to the eastside.
stories.body:
If you've been wondering what's going on with that big development near East Lake Golf Club, wonder no more. Robert Montwaid, who created the legendary Gansevoort Market in NYC, is opening Vela Park Food Works in mid-April, and it sounds like it's going to be a serious addition to the eastside food scene.

This isn't some basic food court situation. We're talking six different chef-driven concepts under one roof, plus craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor seating. Montwaid knows how to do food halls right - his NYC operation has been a destination for years, and his two previous Atlanta ventures have been solid.

The timing feels right too. The eastside has been hungry for more upscale dining options that aren't in Little Five Points or Inman Park proper. Being tucked into the Vela Park rental community means it'll have a built-in customer base, but the real test will be whether it can draw people from across the city like Ponce City Market does.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The pop-up-turned-permanent spot serves Peking chicken platters, soft shell crab bao, and Thai tea martinis. Both chefs are industry veterans - Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC before moving to Atlanta.
stories.body:
After making the rounds as a pop-up, Mushi Ni finally has a permanent home on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, right by the Freedom Park Trail. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened today with a menu that's all about Asian-American comfort food done right.

The Peking chicken platter that feeds two for $25 is already getting attention, along with their soft shell crab bao and umami fries. Jimenez brings some serious credentials from her time working with Gordon Ramsay in New York, and you can taste that level of technique in the execution. The Thai tea martinis are a nice touch too - not something you see everywhere.

The location is smart, catching people coming off the trail and close enough to Inman Park's main drag to get foot traffic. It's the kind of neighborhood spot that could become a regular rotation place if they nail the consistency.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style Brisket to Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue's new Ansley Mall location is unlike anything you've seen - six 22-foot smokers in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Pitmaster John Lewis runs them 24 hours a day in staggered shifts, producing some of the best brisket in Atlanta from tanks he helped weld himself.
stories.body:
John Lewis has built something special at Ansley Mall, and you can see it from the street. That upstairs loft with the panoramic windows? That's where six 22-foot smokers work around the clock, looking like the fanciest engine room in Georgia. Lewis helped weld those smokers from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they're turning out brisket that rivals anything you'll find in the city.

The setup is pure theater - you can watch the whole operation while walking across the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. It's a smart location move, catching both the Ansley Mall crowd and BeltLine traffic. The 24-hour smoking schedule means they're always working on the next batch, factory-style precision that keeps the quality consistent.

Lewis brings credentials from Austin and Charleston, and you can taste that pedigree in every bite. The loaded platter balances smoke and heat just right, and having it this close to the BeltLine makes it a perfect stop during a trail run or weekend stroll.

stories.headline: ICE Agents at Atlanta Airport Mostly Standing Around, Travelers Say
stories.summary: Federal immigration agents are walking the terminals at Hartsfield-Jackson as the government shutdown continues, but passengers say they're not really helping with the chaos. TSA agents are working without pay, leading to massive lines and four-hour waits. The ICE agents are supposed to provide security support, but their actual role remains unclear.
stories.body:
If you've been through Hartsfield-Jackson lately, you've probably seen them: ICE agents in tactical gear walking around the terminals. They're supposed to be helping with security as TSA agents work without pay during the shutdown, but travelers are pretty blunt about what they're actually accomplishing. 'They can't do anything,' seems to be the consensus.

The agents aren't doing security screenings or immigration enforcement - they're basically just providing a visible federal presence while TSA workers deal with massive lines and missed paychecks. Some passengers reported four-hour waits earlier this week, though things seemed to ease up a bit by Tuesday morning. The whole situation is a mess, and adding more federal agents who can't actually process travelers isn't exactly solving the core problem.

Mayor Dickens assured everyone that these agents won't be doing immigration enforcement during this deployment, but their presence is still raising eyebrows. When you're stuck in a line that snakes through multiple terminals, seeing armed agents walking around doesn't exactly speed things up.

stories.headline: City Council Advances Ban on ICE Detention Facilities
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions that would bar ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and require documentation when ICE agents work with local police. The full council votes on April 20. Dozens of residents also criticized Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighters' union contract that council ratified last year.
stories.body:
Atlanta's getting serious about limiting ICE operations in the city. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced two resolutions that would ban detention facilities within city limits and require Atlanta police to document any interactions with immigration agents. It's headed to the full council for a vote on April 20.

The meeting also became a platform for criticizing Mayor Dickens over the firefighters' union contract. The council ratified Atlanta's first-ever firefighter union deal back in April 2025, but Dickens still hasn't signed it. Dozens of residents showed up to ask why, turning what should have been a routine committee meeting into a broader discussion about labor relations.

Both issues reflect growing tensions between federal immigration enforcement and local governance. The ICE resolutions are largely symbolic since the city doesn't directly control federal facilities, but they send a clear message about Atlanta's priorities. The firefighter contract situation is more concrete - it's about whether the mayor will honor agreements the council has already approved.

stories.headline: Park Pride Conference Tackles Climate Resilience in Atlanta Parks
stories.summary: The 25th annual Parks & Greenspace Conference brought hundreds to the Atlanta Botanical Garden to discuss resilient parks in a changing climate. The Southeast's largest parks conference focused on how green spaces can help communities adapt to extreme heat, especially in underserved neighborhoods where urban heat islands are getting worse.
stories.body:
Maybe no city is a climate refuge, as Park Pride's Eli Dickerson put it, but that doesn't mean we can't fight back. The 25th Parks & Greenspace Conference packed the Atlanta Botanical Garden with hundreds of attendees from across the Southeast, all focused on one big question: how do we build resilient parks for a changing climate?

The urgency is real. Atlanta's tree canopy, which earned us the 'city in a forest' nickname, isn't equally distributed. Underserved communities are experiencing dangerous urban heat islands as temperatures climb and green space remains scarce. Extreme heat is already the leading cause of weather-related deaths in the U.S., making this more than just an environmental issue.

What's encouraging is seeing so many people from different states come together to share solutions. From Massachusetts to Minnesota to metro Atlanta, park advocates are learning from each other about everything from flood management to community cooling centers. It's the kind of collaboration we need as climate impacts get harder to ignore.
stories.imageAlt: Attendees at the Parks & Greenspace Conference
